Author: _luke_w_
Content: I think my employer has used IMEC for MPW tapeouts on non-cutting-edge TSMC (e.g. 40LP): https://www.imeciclink.com/en/asic-fabrication/mpw-schedules-2025

Author: _luke_w_
Content: it's more of a "send an email and talk to a guy" experience rather than an online shopping experience

Author: _luke_w_
Content: also I don't think just booking an MPW slot is enough to actually get your hands on the design rule manual, foundry cell libraries and foundry RAM compiler etc for your process. That part probably involves talking directly to TSMC. I've luckily been insulated from this 😅

Author: _luke_w_
Content: The real magic of wafer.space for me so far has been that the PDK is open, and equally importantly that people like Leo have done a ton of work to put a baseline flow together so you can get to GDS on day 1 and then iterate from there

Author: mithro_
Content:
BTW Most foundries have MPW programs. My understand is that most of these MPW programs *lose* the foundry money which leads to them being very restrictive about what you can do with their MPW program.

Both EuroPractice / MOSIS also **lose** money and can only continue to run with government grants and such.

Author: mithro_
Content: I did this comparison back in 2022 - https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rGOHsDU8XALX7jKw31Q59y3njZ0865T2qZPzhHIjIYo/edit?tab=t.0

Author: mithro_
Content: One of my goals with wafer.space is to show that you can run an MPW service for profit (with both the MPW company is making money, the foundry is making money) and then slowly take over the rest of GF's MPW program.

Author: mithro_
Content: (goals might be the wrong word -- maybe dreams?)

Author: mithro_
Content: My (very limited) understanding is their current MPW program consumes somewhere between $50m and $100m in ongoing funding every year.

Author: polyfractal

Author: logic_destroyer
Content:
@Tim 'mithro' Ansell @Leo Moser (mole99) 
It’s crazy — I started my gate-level simulation on **December 3rd**, and **18 days later**, today **December 21st**, I can finally see the first kernel messages… maybe in **two weeks** it’ll boot into the console.

Author: logic_destroyer
Content: This gate-level simulation runs against the Micron SDRAM model, so we’re simulating real SDRAM accesses.

Author: logic_destroyer
Content: We have two caches: a 2-way set-associative instruction cache and a direct-mapped data cache. We also have 4-way I-TLB and 4-way D-TLB MMU caches.

Author: logic_destroyer
Content: I can’t believe that 300k lines of Verilog generated by Yosys (thanx @Lofty) can actually work… that’s crazy.

Author: logic_destroyer
Content: @Leo Moser (mole99)  helped a lot with LibreLane, and his name is immortalized on the chip. Thanks, Leo Moser.

Author: logic_destroyer
Content:
Linux booting is great, but that’s an integration milestone, not verification.
Nobody runs full OS workloads in gate level simulation. That’s what FPGA or emulation is for (Shen & Lipasti).
https://books.google.de/books/about/Modern_Processor_Design.html?id=VIWLAAAACAAJ&redir_esc=y

Author: ravenslofty

Author: ravenslofty
Content: but...two transistors in parallel behave identically to a single transistor that is twice as wide

Author: ravenslofty
Content: and this isn't just theoretical

Author: ravenslofty
Content:
```
.SUBCKT gf180mcu_fd_sc_mcu7t5v0__inv_1 I ZN VDD VNW VPW VSS
X_i_0 ZN I VSS VPW nfet_05v0 W=8.2e-07 L=6e-07
X_i_1 ZN I VDD VNW pfet_05v0 W=1.22e-06 L=5e-07
.ENDS
```
here's a size-1 inverter

Author: ravenslofty
Content:
```
.SUBCKT gf180mcu_fd_sc_mcu7t5v0__inv_2 I ZN VDD VNW VPW VSS
X_i_0_0 ZN I VSS VPW nfet_05v0 W=8.2e-07 L=6e-07
X_i_0_1 VSS I ZN VPW nfet_05v0 W=8.2e-07 L=6e-07
X_i_1_0 ZN I VDD VNW pfet_05v0 W=1.22e-06 L=5e-07
X_i_1_1 VDD I ZN VNW pfet_05v0 W=1.22e-06 L=5e-07
.ENDS
```
here's a size-2 inverter

Author: ravenslofty
Content: so, do we say that the size-2 inverter has 4 transistors and the size-1 inverter has 2?

Author: ravenslofty

Author: ravenslofty
Content: I don't know, I think "number of transistors" is not a useful metric
